> **You:** Kann ich mit Karte zahlen?
> *(Can I pay with a card?)*
> 
> **Cashier:** Ja, nehmen Sie Kreditkarten. Bitte die PIN eingeben.
> *(Yes, we take credit cards. Please enter the PIN.)*
> 
> *(You enter your PIN)*
> 
> **Cashier:** Brauchen Sie eine Tüte?
> *(Do you need a bag?)*
> 
> **You:** Nein, danke. Ich habe eine Tasche.
> *(No, thanks. I have a bag.)*
> 
> **Cashier:** Hier ist Ihre Quittung. Schönen Tag noch!
> *(Here is your receipt. Have a nice day!)*
> 
> **You:** Danke, gleichfalls!
> *(Thanks, same to you!)*

Notice the phrase **Schönen Tag noch!** (Have a nice day!). This is the standard, polite way a cashier will end a transaction in Germany. The best response is **Danke, gleichfalls** (Thanks, likewise/the same to you), which you learned in Chapter 2.

## Quick Reference: Shopping Vocabulary

Here is a quick glossary of the most important terms from this chapter to keep on hand while you travel:

*   **die Größe** – size
*   **die Farbe** – color
*   **die Umkleidekabine** – fitting room
*   **anprobieren** – to try on (clothing)
*   **die Kasse** – checkout / cash register
*   **die Quittung** – receipt
*   **die Tüte** – plastic/paper bag
*   **die Tasche** – reusable shopping bag or purse
*   **bar** – cash (as a payment method: "Ich zahle bar")
*   **die EC-Karte** – debit card (commonly used in Germany)
*   **die Kreditkarte** – credit card
*   **das Wechselgeld** – change (the coins returned to you)
*   **der Einkaufswagen** – shopping cart
*   **der Einkaufskorb** – shopping basket

## Key Takeaways

*   When you need help finding an item, use **Wo finde ich...?** (Where do I find...?) or **Haben Sie...?** (Do you have...?).
*   To ask for clothing in a different size or color, ask **Haben Sie das in einer anderen Größe/Farbe?** Remember that Germany uses EU sizing, so check your conversions before you shop.
*   Always bring your own shopping bags to German stores, and be prepared to pay a small fee if you need to buy a plastic bag at the register.
*   At the supermarket, remember to bring a coin (usually 1 Euro or 2 Euro) to unlock a shopping cart, and be sure to weigh your own produce in the department before going to the checkout.
*   Germany is still somewhat cash-oriented. Always carry some cash, but you can ask **Nehmen Sie Kreditkarten?** to find out if you can use your card.
*   When checking out, listen for **Das macht...** (That makes...), **Brauchen Sie eine Tüte?** (Do you need a bag?), and **Schönen Tag noch!** (Have a nice day!). Respond with **Danke, gleichfalls!** (Thanks, same to you!).

modules.title: Sightseeing and Telling Time
modules.content:
## Asking About Opening Hours

You are standing in front of the Brandenburg Gate in Berlin, or perhaps gazing up at the Kölner Dom (Cologne Cathedral). You check your watch and realize it is getting late, and you want to visit the nearby museum before you head back to your hotel. But when does it close? 

In Chapter 3, you learned how to count and use numbers for paying. In Chapter 2, you learned how to ask basic questions. Now, we are going to combine those skills so you can plan your sightseeing itinerary effectively. 

To ask when a place opens, you use the phrase:
**Wann öffnet...?** (When does ... open?)

To ask when a place closes, you use:
**Wann schließt...?** (When does ... close?)

If you want to ask about a specific museum or church, you simply put the name of the place at the end of the sentence. 

*   *Wann öffnet das Museum?* (When does the museum open?)
*   *Wann schließt die Kirche?* (When does the church close?)

### The Magic Word: "Geöffnet"

When you look at a sign on a door or ask a staff member about hours, you will frequently hear or see the word **geöffnet**. This is the German equivalent of "open" in the context of business hours. 

A very common phrase you will see on signs is:
**Wir haben geöffnet von... bis...** (We are open from... to...)

Since you already know your numbers from Chapter 3, you just need to slot them into this phrase. 

*   *Wir haben geöffnet von neun bis fünf.* (We are open from nine to five.)
*   *Wir haben geöffnet von zehn bis achtzehn.* (We are open from ten to eighteen. *Note: Germans frequently use the 24-hour clock, which you will learn more about shortly.*)

If you want to ask a staff member directly, "Are you open today?", you can say:
**Sind Sie heute geöffnet?**

## Telling Time on the Hour and Half-Hour

To understand opening hours and tour schedules, you need to know how to tell time in German. Let's start with the basics: the hour and the half-hour.

### Asking for the Time

To ask a stranger for the time, you use a slightly longer, polite phrase:
**Wie spät ist es?** (How late is it? / What time is it?)

### On the Hour

When the time is exactly on the hour (e.g., 3:00), you use the word **Uhr** (clock/o'clock). You say the number, followed by *Uhr*.

*   8:00 = *acht Uhr*
*   12:00 = *zwölf Uhr*
*   14:00 = *vierzehn Uhr*

### The Half-Hour

This is where German differs slightly from English. In English, 3:30 is "three-thirty" or "half past three." In German, when you look at a clock, you are looking at the hour that is *coming up*. Therefore, 3:30 is considered "half of the fourth hour." 

You use the phrase **halb** (half) followed by the *next* hour.

*   3:30 = *halb vier* (literally: half four)
*   7:30 = *halb acht* (literally: half eight)
*   12:30 = *halb eins* (literally: half one)

This can take a moment to get used to. Just remember: if a tour starts at *halb vier*, it starts at 3:30, not 4:30!

### The 24-Hour Clock (Military Time)

When dealing with train schedules (as you saw in Chapter 4), museum opening hours, and tour tickets, Germans almost exclusively use the 24-hour clock to avoid morning/evening confusion. 

To say a time in the afternoon or evening using the 24-hour clock, you simply say the number as it is written, followed by *Uhr*.

*   14:00 = *vierzehn Uhr* (2:00 PM)
*   16:30 = *halb fünf* or *sechzehn Uhr dreißig* (4:30 PM)
*   19:00 = *neunzehn Uhr* (7:00 PM)

If you are reading a ticket or a sign, seeing 18:00 means you say *achtzehn Uhr*. 

## Buying Tickets for Tours and Events

Whether you are visiting a world-class art gallery, taking a boat cruise down the Rhine River, or booking a guided walking tour through Munich, you will need to buy a ticket. 

The German word for ticket is **das Ticket** (or **die Eintrittskarte** for an admission ticket to a museum/attraction). Since *Ticket* is an English loanword, it is very easy to remember.

### Key Vocabulary for Buying Tickets

Before we look at a scenario, here are a few essential words you will need:
*   **die Führung** - guided tour
*   **die Ausstellung** - exhibition
*   **das Museum** - museum
*   **erwachsen** - adult (adjective)
*   **ermäßigt** - reduced (often used for students, seniors, or children)
*   **kostenlos** - free (of charge)

### A Realistic Scenario: At the Museum Ticket Counter

Let's look at a conversation between you (the traveler) and a ticket agent at an art museum. You want to buy a ticket, find out when the guided tour is, and check the closing time.

**You:** *Guten Tag. Ich möchte eine Eintrittskarte, bitte.* 
(Good day. I would like an admission ticket, please.)

**Agent:** *Für einen Erwachsenen?* 
(For one adult?)

**You:** *Ja. Gibt es eine Führung heute?* 
(Yes. Is there a guided tour today?)

**Agent:** *Ja, um vierzehn Uhr dreißig.* 
(Yes, at 14:30 / 2:30 PM.)

**You:** *Wann schließt das Museum?* 
(When does the museum close?)

**Agent:** *Wir haben geöffnet von zehn bis achtzehn Uhr.* 
(We are open from ten to eighteen / 6:00 PM.)

**You:** *Perfekt. Ich nehme die Eintrittskarte und ein Ticket für die Führung.* 
(Perfect. I will take the admission ticket and a ticket for the tour.)

**Agent:** *Das macht achtzehn Euro, bitte.* 
(That makes eighteen euros, please.)

Notice how you used *Ich möchte* (I would like) from Chapter 7 (Shopping), and the agent used numbers and the 24-hour clock. By combining these foundational skills, you successfully secured your museum entry and tour!

## Asking if Photography is Allowed

One of the most important cultural rules to learn when sightseeing in Germany is regarding photography. While Germans love photography, they are also fiercely protective of privacy and copyright. 

In many art museums, taking photographs of the artwork is strictly prohibited due to copyright laws. In historical churches, flash photography may be banned to protect old tapestries and paintings from light damage. Furthermore, if you want to take a picture of a person, it is polite—and sometimes legally required—to ask for their permission first.

To ask if you are allowed to take photographs, you use the phrase:
**Darf ich fotografieren?** (Am I allowed to take photographs?)

Alternatively, if you want to ask if photography is allowed in general, you can say:
**Ist Fotografieren erlaubt?** (Is photography allowed?)

### Understanding the Response

The staff member will respond with one of two words:
*   **Ja** (Yes) - You are good to go!
*   **Nein** (No) - Put the camera away.

If the answer is no, they might say:
*   *Nein, Fotografieren ist verboten.* (No, photography is forbidden.)

If the answer is yes, but with a condition, they might say:
*   *Ja, aber ohne Blitz.* (Yes, but without flash.)
*   *Ja, aber nur ohne Stativ.* (Yes, but only without a tripod. *Note: Some places ban tripods because they create tripping hazards in crowded rooms.*)

### A Quick Note on the Word "Verboten"

You may have heard the word **verboten** used in English to mean "strictly forbidden." In German, it simply means "forbidden" or "prohibited." You will see this word on signs all over Germany—on construction fences, in museums, and in parks. If you see a sign with a camera icon and a red line through it, accompanied by the word *Fotografieren verboten*, do not take pictures.

## Putting It All Together

Imagine you are planning your final afternoon in Dresden. You want to visit the Zwinger, a famous palace complex that houses several art museums. You walk up to the information desk to gather your facts before committing to a ticket.

**You:** *Guten Tag. Wann öffnet das Museum?*
(Hello. When does the museum open?)

**Agent:** *Wir haben heute von zehn bis achtzehn Uhr geöffnet.*
(We are open today from ten to eighteen / 6:00 PM.)

**You:** *Darf ich in den Ausstellungen fotografieren?*
(Am I allowed to take photographs in the exhibitions?)

**Agent:** *Nein, Fotografieren ist verboten.*
(No, photography is forbidden.)

**You:** *Verstehe. Wann ist die Führung?*
(I understand. When is the guided tour?)

**Agent:** *Die Führung ist um vierzehn Uhr.*
(The tour is at 14:00 / 2:00 PM.)

**You:** *Gut. Ich möchte ein Ticket für die Führung, bitte.*
(Good. I would like a ticket for the tour, please.)

In this short exchange, you successfully asked for opening hours, checked the photography policy, confirmed the tour time, and purchased your ticket. 

## Key Takeaways

*   **Opening Hours:** Ask *Wann öffnet...?* (When does it open?) and *Wann schließt...?* (When does it close?). Look for the word **geöffnet** (open) on signs.
*   **Telling Time:** Use **Uhr** for "o'clock" (e.g., *drei Uhr* = 3:00). Use **halb** for the half-hour, but remember it looks forward to the *next* hour (e.g., *halb vier* = 3:30, not 4:30).
*   **24-Hour Clock:** Germans use the 24-hour clock for official schedules, tickets, and opening hours. 3:00 PM is *fünfzehn Uhr*.
*   **Buying Tickets:** Ask for **die Eintrittskarte** (admission ticket) or **das Ticket für die Führung** (ticket for the guided tour). 
*   **Photography:** Always ask **Darf ich fotografieren?** or **Ist Fotografieren erlaubt?** before taking pictures in museums or churches. If you see **Fotografieren verboten**, keep your camera put away.

modules.title: Emergencies and Health
modules.content:
## The Most Important Number: 112

You’re walking through a train station in Munich. Suddenly, the person next to you collapses. People around you start to react, but everyone is speaking rapid German. You need to get help immediately. 

In a medical emergency or accident, you don't need to build complex sentences. You need three things: your phone, the right number, and a few critical words. 

In Germany, Austria, and Switzerland, the European-wide emergency number is **112**. 

Dialing 112 connects you to emergency services. It is free, works from any phone (even without a SIM card), and is available 24/7. The dispatcher who answers will speak German, but they are trained to handle calls in English as well. 

If you are in a non-medical emergency and need the police directly, you can dial **110**. However, if you are unsure which one you need, dial 112. The dispatchers can route your call to the police if necessary.

### What to Say on the Phone

When the operator answers, they will say the equivalent of "Emergency services, what is your emergency?" You need to state what happened and where you are.

Here are the most critical emergency words. Notice that all German nouns are capitalized, a rule you’ve seen throughout this book.

*   **Hilfe!** (Help!)
*   **Notfall** (Emergency)
*   **Arzt** (Doctor - *Note: The 'r' is barely pronounced, making it sound almost like "Ahtst".*)
*   **Krankenhaus** (Hospital)
*   **Polizei** (Police)
*   **Feuer** (Fire)

To report an accident, you can say:
*   **Ein Unfall!** (An accident!)
*   **Ich brauche einen Arzt.** (I need a doctor.)

Remember the numbers and navigation vocabulary from *Navigation and Public Transportation*. The operator will need to know your location. Use the phrase:
*   **Ich bin in...** (I am in/at...) followed by your location. For example: *Ich bin in der Berliner Straße.* (I am on Berliner Street.)

### Asking for English in an Urgent Situation

In a high-stress situation, you may not have the bandwidth to speak German. As mentioned, 112 operators generally speak English. But if you are dealing with a bystander, a security guard, or a receptionist, you need to ask them to switch languages. 

Pulling from the polite phrases you learned in *Polite Phrases and Simple Questions*, you can ask:

*   **Sprechen Sie Englisch?** (Do you speak English?)
*   **Bitte rufen Sie 112 an.** (Please call 112. - *Literally: Please call 112 up.*)
*   **Können Sie mir helfen?** (Can you help me?)
*   **Ich brauche einen Arzt, der Englisch spricht.** (I need a doctor who speaks English.)

## Finding a Pharmacy

Not every medical issue requires a hospital visit or an ambulance. For minor ailments—like a headache, a stomach bug, or a small cut—you need a pharmacy.

In German-speaking countries, a pharmacy is called an **Apotheke**. You will recognize it by a large red "A" sign on the outside of the building. 

It is important not to confuse an *Apotheke* with a **Drogerie** (drugstore). As you may have noticed while *Shopping and Buying Souvenirs*, a *Drogerie* (like the popular chains dm or Rossmann) sells shampoo, toothpaste, and household goods, but it does **not** sell medication. To get medicine like ibuprofen or cough syrup, you must go to an *Apotheke*.

### Asking for the Apotheke

If you are out sightseeing and start to feel unwell, use your navigation vocabulary to find the nearest pharmacy:

*   **Wo ist die nächste Apotheke?** (Where is the nearest pharmacy?)
*   **Gibt es hier eine Apotheke?** (Is there a pharmacy here?)

### Inside the Apotheke

German pharmacists are highly trained and can offer advice for minor ailments. To ask for something for a specific problem, use this simple formula:

**Ich brauche etwas gegen [problem].**
*(I need something against [problem].)*

For example:
*   **Ich brauche etwas gegen Kopfschmerzen.** (I need something for a headache.)
*   **Ich brauche etwas gegen Halsschmerzen.** (I need something for a sore throat.)

If you have a fever, you can say:
*   **Ich habe Fieber.** (I have a fever.)

If you need bandages for a cut:
*   **Ich brauche Pflaster.** (I need band-aids/plasters.)

## Explaining Basic Medical Symptoms

If you end up at a clinic or speaking to a doctor, you will need to explain what is wrong. German has a very logical way of constructing words for body parts and pains, making it relatively easy to learn the basics.

### The Rule of Pain

The German word for pain is **Schmerzen**. To say "I have pain," you use the phrase **Ich habe...** (I have...) followed by the body part and the word *Schmerzen*.

Here are the most common body parts:
*   **Kopf** (Head)
*   **Bauch** (Stomach)
*   **Rücken** (Back)
*   **Hals** (Throat/Neck)
*   **Zahn** (Tooth)

Combine them like this:
*   **Ich habe Kopfschmerzen.** (I have a headache.)
*   **Ich habe Bauchschmerzen.** (I have a stomachache.)
*   **Ich habe Rückenschmerzen.** (I have back pain.)
*   **Ich habe Halsschmerzen.** (I have a sore throat.)
*   **Ich habe Zahnschmerzen.** (I have a toothache.)

### "My Body Part Hurts"

Another way to express pain is to say that a specific body part hurts. The verb for "to hurt" or "to ache" is **wehtun**. 

In German, the sentence structure flips: the body part becomes the subject of the sentence, and the person experiencing the pain is in the dative case. You don't need to dive deep into grammar right now; just memorize the pattern:

**Mein [body part] tut weh.** (My [body part] hurts.)

*   **Mein Kopf tut weh.** (My head hurts.)
*   **Mein Bauch tut weh.** (My stomach hurts.)
*   **Mein Rücken tut weh.** (My back hurts.)

### Nausea and Other Common Symptoms

If you feel sick to your stomach or like you might vomit, use these phrases:
*   **Mir ist übel.** (I feel nauseous / I feel sick.)
*   **Ich muss mich übergeben.** (I need to vomit / throw up.)

For general sickness or feeling unwell:
*   **Ich bin krank.** (I am sick.)
*   **Ich fühle mich nicht gut.** (I don't feel well.)

If you are experiencing a more severe symptom like chest pain or shortness of breath, it is critical to be direct:
*   **Brustschmerzen** (Chest pain - *Ich habe Brustschmerzen.*)
*   **Ich bekomme keine Luft.** (I can't breathe. - *Literally: I get no air.*)

### Allergies

If you have a severe allergy, you must be able to communicate it quickly. The word for allergy is **Allergie**, and the word for "allergic to" is **allergisch gegen**.

*   **Ich bin allergisch gegen...** (I am allergic to...)
*   **Ich bin allergisch gegen Nüsse.** (I am allergic to nuts.)
*   **Ich bin allergisch gegen Penicillin.** (I am allergic to penicillin.)

If you have a severe allergic reaction and need an ambulance, say:
*   **Ich habe einen allergischen Schock.** (I am having anaphylactic shock.)

## A Realistic Scenario: At the Pharmacy

Let’s put these phrases together into a concrete scenario. Imagine you are traveling in Vienna, and you wake up with a terrible stomachache and a fever. You find a nearby *Apotheke* and walk inside.

**Pharmacist:** Guten Tag! Was kann ich für Sie tun?
*(Hello! What can I do for you?)*

**You:** Guten Tag. Ich brauche Hilfe. Sprechen Sie Englisch?
*(Hello. I need help. Do you speak English?)*

**Pharmacist:** Ein bisschen. Was ist das Problem?
*(A little bit. What is the problem?)*

**You:** Ich habe Bauchschmerzen und ich habe Fieber. Mir ist auch übel.
*(I have a stomachache and I have a fever. I am also nauseous.)*

**Pharmacist:** Seit wann haben Sie die Symptome?
*(Since when have you had the symptoms?)*

**You:** Seit gestern. 
*(Since yesterday.)*

**Pharmacist:** Okay. Ich gebe Ihnen etwas gegen die Schmerzen und gegen die Übelkeit. Trinken Sie viel Wasser.
*(Okay. I will give you something against the pain and against the nausea. Drink a lot of water.)*

**You:** Danke schön. Was kostet das?
*(Thank you very much. How much does that cost? - *Using your numbers and paying skills from Chapter 3.*)*)

**Pharmacist:** Das macht 12 Euro 50.
*(That makes 12 euros 50.)*

## Key Vocabulary Pronunciation Guide

As covered in *Pronunciation and Basic Greetings*, German vowels are generally "pure," and certain letter combinations have specific sounds. Here is a quick guide to pronouncing the medical terms in this chapter:

*   **Arzt** (Doctor): The 'r' is vocalized softly at the back of the throat, and the 't' is sharp. It sounds almost like "Ah-tst".
*   **Schmerzen** (Pain): The **sch** combination is pronounced like the English "sh" (as in *shoe*). The 'e' is a short, crisp vowel. Pronounced: "SHMERT-sen".
*   **Übel** (Nauseous): Uses the **ü** (Umlaut U) sound. To pronounce it, say the English word "ee" and then round your lips tightly as if you are whistling. 
*   **Apotheke** (Pharmacy): The 'e' at the end is pronounced (it is not silent like in English). The 'th' is pronounced simply as a 't'. Pronounced: "Ah-po-TEH-keh".
*   **Zahn** (Tooth): The 'z' at the beginning of a German word is pronounced like a 'ts' sound. Pronounced: "TSahn".

## When You Need the Police

While medical emergencies are the most common reason travelers need help, you might also need to interact with the police if you are the victim of a crime, such as pickpocketing, or if you witness an accident.

To call the police directly in Germany, dial **110**. (In Austria and Switzerland, 112 will also connect you to the police, but 110 works in Germany specifically). 

If you approach a police officer on the street, you can say:
*   **Hilfe!** (Help!)
*   **Ich brauche die Polizei.** (I need the police.)

To report that something was stolen:
*   **Mein Portemonnaie ist weg.** (My wallet is gone.)
*   **Mein Pass ist gestohlen.** (My passport is stolen. - *Remember this from Accommodation and Checking In when discussing your passport.*)
*   **Diebe!** (Thieves!)

If you are in an accident and need to file a report for insurance purposes, you can ask the officer:
*   **Können Sie mir helfen?** (Can you help me?)
*   **Sprechen Sie Englisch?** (Do you speak English?)

## Key Takeaways

*   **112 is your lifeline:** Dial 112 for any medical emergency or fire. It is free and operates 24/7 across Europe. Dial 110 for the police in Germany.
*   **Apotheke vs. Drogerie:** Go to an **Apotheke** (red "A" sign) for medication and medical advice. A *Drogerie* only sells toiletries and household goods.
*   **The Pain Formula:** To explain aches and pains, combine the body part with the word *Schmerzen*. (e.g., *Kopf* + *Schmerzen* = *Ich habe Kopfschmerzen*).
*   **The Hurt Formula:** To say a specific body part hurts, use *Mein [body part] tut weh.* (e.g., *Mein Bauch tut weh.*).
*   **Ask for English:** In an urgent situation, it is entirely acceptable to ask, *Sprechen Sie Englisch?* Accuracy and speed are more important than trying to use complex German grammar.
*   **Nausea and Fever:** Use *Mir ist übel* for nausea and *Ich habe Fieber* for a fever.

9. [Chapter 9: Emergencies and Health](#chapter-9-emergencies-and-health)
- [Summary](#summary)
- [Glossary](#glossary)

## Introduction

### Welcome and Purpose

*Willkommen!* Welcome to your German language journey. If you have picked up this book, it means you are likely planning an adventure to a beautiful German-speaking country. Perhaps you are dreaming of wandering through the cobblestone streets of Munich, marveling at the architectural symphony of Vienna, or exploring the breathtaking, snow-capped peaks of the Swiss Alps. Maybe you are envisioning yourself sipping a perfectly poured beer in a lively Bavarian beer garden, or indulging in a warm, freshly baked pretzel at a bustling Christmas market. 

Whatever your travel dreams entail, there is a profound difference between simply visiting a country and truly experiencing it. The bridge between those two experiences is language. 

The purpose of this book is simple: to equip you with the essential German you need to travel confidently, connect authentically with locals, and navigate your journey smoothly, all without the overwhelm of traditional language programs. You do not need to conjugate every verb in the subjunctive mood or memorize complex grammar tables to have a meaningful trip. You just need the right words, the right phrases, and the confidence to use them. This book is designed to be your friendly, pocket-sized mentor, guiding you step-by-step through the linguistic landscape of travel German so that you can focus on what really matters—enjoying your trip.

### What Readers Will Learn

When you set out to learn a new language, it is easy to feel intimidated. German, in particular, has a reputation for long compound words and strict rules. But here is the secret: for travel, you only need a fraction of the language. By focusing on high-frequency situations and practical vocabulary, you will be amazed at how quickly you can make yourself understood. 

In this book, you will learn exactly what you need to handle the most common scenarios you will face on the road. We will start by getting your mouth around German pronunciation—those unique vowel combinations and that famous "ch" sound—so you can speak clearly and be understood the first time. You will master the art of basic greetings, from a cheerful "Guten Morgen" to a polite "Guten Abend."

From there, we will dive into the social fabric of the language. You will learn how to be a gracious guest by mastering polite phrases like "Bitte" (please/you're welcome) and "Danke" (thank you), along with simple questions that will become your linguistic Swiss Army knife. You will learn how to count, understand prices, and confidently handle money, ensuring you never overpay for a souvenir or get shortchanged at a bakery. 

Navigation is a breeze when you know how to ask for directions to the train station or understand when a bus is arriving. You will learn the vocabulary needed to check into your hotel smoothly, order a bratwurst and a coffee without pointing and gesturing, and shop for unique gifts to bring back home. We will also cover how to ask for the time so you never miss a museum opening, and perhaps most importantly, you will learn how to handle emergencies and health issues, giving you peace of mind should the unexpected occur. You are not just learning words; you are learning how to thrive abroad.

### Book Structure

To make your learning process as smooth and efficient as possible, this book is structured like a journey. We have broken down the vast German language into a focused roadmap of nine essential themes. Think of these chapters as stepping stones. Each one builds upon the last, gradually expanding your comfort zone. 

We will begin at the very beginning: **Pronunciation and Basic Greetings**. Here, we lay the foundation, ensuring you feel comfortable making the sounds of the language and can say hello and goodbye with confidence. Next, we move to **Polite Phrases and Simple Questions**. Politeness goes a very long way in German-speaking cultures, and learning a few courtesies will instantly open doors for you.

From there, we enter the practical day-to-day mechanics of travel. You will learn about **Numbers, Money, and Paying**, which is crucial for everything from buying a tram ticket to paying for dinner. This naturally flows into **Navigation and Public Transportation**, where you will learn how to master the U-Bahn and ask for directions. 

Once you can get around, you will need a place to stay. The **Accommodation and Checking In** chapter will walk you through hotel vocabulary and interacting with front desk staff. Then comes one of the most enjoyable parts of travel: **Dining Out and Ordering Food**. You will learn how to ask for the menu, order local specialties, and request the bill. 

After dining, we will explore **Shopping and Buying Souvenirs**, giving you the tools to browse boutiques and markets. We will then broaden your horizons with **Sightseeing and Telling Time**, helping you plan your days around museum visits and historical landmarks. Finally, because safety is paramount, we conclude with **Emergencies and Health**, equipping you with the vocabulary to seek help, visit a pharmacy, or describe an ailment if needed.

Each chapter is designed to be bite-sized and digestible. You can read the book cover to cover before your trip, or use it as a quick reference guide while you are on the road. 

### Motivation

Learning a language is an act of courage. It requires you to step out of your comfort zone and make mistakes. But let me assure you: every single attempt you make to speak German will be worth it. 

There is a magical moment that happens when you travel. It occurs when you walk into a local bakery, make eye contact with the person behind the counter, and instead of defaulting to English, you say, "Einen Kaffee, bitte." The clerk’s face lights up. They smile. In that instant, you are no longer just a tourist passing through; you are a traveler engaging with their culture. That tiny interaction, that brief connection, is the heartbeat of travel. 

Do not worry about having a perfect accent. Do not worry about mixing up your "der," "die," and "das." Locals do not expect perfection from travelers; they appreciate the effort. The fact that you are trying to speak their language shows respect, humility, and a genuine interest in their world. That effort is almost always rewarded with warmer service, helpful tips, and a much richer travel experience.

You are capable of doing this. You have already taken the hardest step by deciding to learn. As you turn the pages of this book, remember that you are not just memorizing vocabulary; you are unlocking experiences. You are preparing to navigate new cities with confidence, savor new flavors, and create memories that will last a lifetime. 

So, take a deep breath, turn the page, and let’s begin. Your German adventure starts right now. *Viel Erfolg!* (Much success!)

# Chapter 1: Pronunciation and Basic Greetings

## Why German Is Easier Than It Looks

You step into a bakery in Berlin. The display is full of pretzels, brötchen, and layered cakes. You want to order, but the words on the signs look like a jumble of impossibly long consonants. You take a deep breath, point, and try your best. The baker smiles, hands you your pastry, and says something that sounds incredibly warm and welcoming. You have just survived your first German interaction.

For many travelers, German looks intimidating on paper. Words like *Streichholzschächtelchen* (little matchbox) seem to require superhuman tongue gymnastics. But here is the secret: German is phonetically consistent. Unlike English, where "read" and "read" can be pronounced two entirely different ways depending on the context, German letters almost always make the exact same sound. Once you learn the rules of the alphabet, you can look at almost any German word and pronounce it correctly on your first try. 

This chapter gives you the foundational building blocks of the language. We will start with the alphabet and the special sounds created by umlauts, move into the most useful greetings for different times of day, and finish with how to introduce yourself. By the end, you will be equipped to walk into that Berlin bakery and greet the baker with confidence.

## The German Alphabet and Vowels

The German alphabet shares the same 26 letters as the English alphabet, but four of those letters are pronounced quite differently. Additionally, German includes four special characters (ä, ö, ü, and ß) that we will cover shortly. 

Let us start with the vowels. In English, vowels are notoriously unpredictable. In German, they are strict and consistent. The most important rule to remember is that **German vowels are generally "pure."** You do not glide from one sound to another within a single vowel like you do in English. 

### Short vs. Long Vowels

German vowels are pronounced either short or long. While there are specific spelling rules that dictate whether a vowel is short or long, for a traveler, the best approach is to listen and mimic. However, knowing the difference between the two sounds is vital.

*   **Short vowels:** These are crisp and clipped. The vowel sound is cut off quickly. 
    *   *Example:* The **a** in *Mann* (man) sounds like the "a" in the English word "father," but much shorter.
    *   *Example:* The **e** in *Bett* (bed) sounds like the "e" in the English word "bed."
    *   *Example:* The **i** in *mit* (with) sounds like the "i" in the English word "sit."
    *   *Example:* The **o** in *Sonne* (sun) sounds like the "o" in the English word "hot."
    *   *Example:* The **u** in *Mutter* (mother) sounds like the "u" in the English word "put."
*   **Long vowels:** These are held for twice as long as a short vowel. You sustain the sound.
    *   *Example:* The **a** in *Tag* (day) sounds like the "a" in "father," but held longer.
    *   *Example:* The **e** in *Leben* (life) sounds like the "ay" in "say," but without the "y" glide at the end. It is a pure, sustained "eh" sound.
    *   *Example:* The **i** in *mir* (to me) sounds like the "ee" in "seen."
    *   *Example:* The **o** in *Sohn* (son) sounds like the "o" in "tone," but pure.
    *   *Example:* The **u** in *Buch* (book) sounds like the "oo" in "moon."

### Tricky Consonants

Most German consonants sound similar to their English counterparts, but a few require special attention. Getting these right will immediately make you sound more like a local.

*   **w:** The German **w** is pronounced like the English **v**. 
    *   *Example:* *Was* (what) is pronounced like "vas."
*   **v:** The German **v** is usually pronounced like the English **f**. 
    *   *Example:* *Vater* (father) is pronounced like "fah-ter."
*   **z:** The German **z** is pronounced like the English **ts**. 
    *   *Example:* *Zeit* (time) is pronounced like "tsite."
*   **s:** At the beginning of a word, **s** is usually pronounced like the English **z**. 
    *   *Example:* *Sonne* (sun) is pronounced like "zohn-uh." In the middle or end of a word, it is usually a sharp "s" like in "bus."
*   **r:** The German **r** is famously difficult for English speakers. It is not rolled on the tip of the tongue like in Spanish, nor is it the soft English "r." Instead, it is a "uvular fricative," meaning it is produced by vibrating the uvula at the very back of the throat—similar to the French "r." If this is too difficult at first, a gentle gargling sound in the back of the throat will do. When **r** comes at the very end of a word, it is often swallowed and sounds almost like a soft "uh" vowel.
    *   *Example:* *Mutter* (mother) is often pronounced closer to "moot-uh."
*   **l:** The German **l** is always clear and crisp, placed further forward in the mouth than the English "l." It never sounds like the dark "l" at the end of the English word "ball."
*   **h:** At the beginning of a word, it is pronounced with a breathy "h" sound, just like in English. However, when **h** follows a vowel in the middle of a word, it is silent and simply tells you to stretch the vowel before it.
    *   *Example:* *gehen* (to go) is pronounced "gay-en," with the "h" completely silent.

## The Umlauts and the Eszett

German features four special characters that do not exist in the standard English alphabet. Mastering these is essential for both pronunciation and reading signs while traveling.

### What is an Umlaut?

An **umlaut** refers to the two dots placed over a vowel (ä, ö, ü). Linguistically, an umlaut changes the way a vowel is pronounced by moving the sound forward in the mouth. For travelers, the easiest way to learn these sounds is to use English approximations.

*   **ä (Umlaut A):** Pronounced like the "e" in the English word "bed," but slightly more open. 
    *   *Example:* *Bär* (bear) sounds roughly like "bare" (but with a flatter "e" sound).
*   **ö (Umlaut O):** This sound does not exist in standard English. To make this sound, say a long English "e" (as in "see"), but round your lips tightly as if you are going to whistle. It sounds similar to the "u" in the British pronunciation of "dirt" or the French "eu." 
    *   *Example:* *schön* (beautiful) sounds roughly like "shurn."
*   **ü (Umlaut U):** This sound also does not exist in English. To make this sound, say a long English "e" (as in "see"), but push your lips forward into a tight circle. It sounds similar to the "oo" in "moon," but formed further forward in the mouth.
    *   *Example:* *über* (over) sounds roughly like "oo-ber," but with the tight, forward lip rounding.

### The Eszett (ß)

The **ß** (pronounced *Eszett*, or "S-Z") is a uniquely German letter that looks like a sharp "B" but is actually a double-s. It is pronounced exactly like a sharp English "s" (as in "snake"). It is only used after long vowels or diphthongs.

*   *Example:* *Straße* (street) is pronounced "shtrah-suh." 
*   *Note for travelers:* In Switzerland and Liechtenstein, the ß is not used at all; they simply write "ss" in its place.

### Letter Combinations

Finally, German features a few letter combinations that produce single, distinct sounds. These appear constantly in greetings and basic words.

*   **ei:** Pronounced like the English word "eye." 
    *   *Example:* *ein* (a/an) sounds like "eye-n."
*   **ie:** Pronounced like the English "ee" in "seen." 
    *   *Example:* *Liebe* (love) sounds like "lee-buh."
*   **eu:** Pronounced like the "oy" in "boy." 
    *   *Example:* *Deutsch* (German) sounds like "doytsh."
*   **sch:** Pronounced like the English "sh" in "shoe." 
    *   *Example:* *Schule* (school) sounds like "shoo-luh."
*   **ch:** This is the other famously difficult German sound. After a, o, u, and au, it is a harsh, throat-clearing sound—like the "ch" in the Scottish word "loch." After all other vowels (e, i, ä, ö, ü) and consonants, it is softer, resembling a hissing "sh" sound.
    *   *Example:* *Buch* (book) uses the harsh throat sound.
    *   *Example:* *ich* (I) uses the softer hissing sound, similar to "ish" but with the tongue positioned differently.

## Greetings for Every Time of Day

Now that you know how the letters sound, let's put them together into actual words. Germans place a high value on politeness, and greeting someone properly when you enter a shop, a restaurant, or an elevator is expected. Failing to say hello when you walk into a small bakery or a doctor's waiting room is considered rude.

German distinguishes between **formal** and **informal** speech. If you are speaking to a stranger, a shopkeeper, or someone older than you, you use the formal register. If you are speaking to friends, family, or children, you use the informal register. 

### The Universal Greeting

The most common, all-purpose greeting in Germany is **Hallo**. It means "Hello" and can be used at any time of day, with anyone, in almost any casual situation. 

*   **Hallo** (Hello) — Pronounced "hah-loh."

### Time-Specific Greetings

For more formal situations, or simply to sound more like a local, Germans use greetings that correspond to the time of day.

*   **Guten Morgen** (Good morning) — Pronounced "goo-ten mor-gen." Used from the early morning until about 10:00 AM or 11:00 AM. 
*   **Guten Tag** (Good day) — Pronounced "goo-ten tahk." Used from late morning until about 6:00 PM. This is your go-to greeting when walking into a shop or approaching a ticket counter during the day.
*   **Guten Abend** (Good evening) — Pronounced "goo-ten ah-bent." Used from roughly 6:00 PM onward.

### Regional Variations

Germany, Austria, and Switzerland have strong regional dialects. While standard German (called *Hochdeutsch*) will be understood everywhere, you will hear regional variations constantly. Using them shows you are paying attention to the local culture.

*   **Grüß Gott** (May God greet you) — Pronounced "gruess got." This is the standard greeting in Bavaria (southern Germany) and Austria. You will hear this constantly in Munich or Salzburg instead of "Guten Tag."
*   **Moin** (Hello) — Pronounced "moy-n." Used primarily in northern Germany, especially around Hamburg and the coast. It can be used at any time of day.
*   **Servus** (Hello / Goodbye) — Pronounced "zair-voos." Common in Bavaria and Austria, used for both arriving and leaving.

### The All-Important "Wie geht es Ihnen?"

After greeting someone, it is common courtesy to ask how they are doing. 

*   **Wie geht es Ihnen?** (How are you? - Formal) — Pronounced "vee gay-t es ee-nen." Use this with shopkeepers, hotel staff, or anyone you do not know well.
*   **Wie geht es dir?** (How are you? - Informal) — Pronounced "vee gay-t es deer." Use this with friends or peers your own age.

*Traveler's Note:* In English-speaking cultures, "How are you?" is often used as a passing greeting, and the expected answer is a brief "Good, you?" In Germany, this question is usually asked with genuine interest, particularly in formal settings. If a German asks "Wie geht es Ihnen?", they often expect a brief, honest answer rather than a reflexive "Fine." However, as a traveler keeping interactions brief, a simple "Danke, gut" (Thanks, good) is a perfectly acceptable response.

## Saying Goodbye

Just as there are specific ways to say hello, there are specific phrases for saying goodbye. The phrase you choose depends on whether you are leaving permanently or just for a while, and whether the situation is formal or informal.

### The Standard Goodbyes

*   **Auf Wiedersehen** (Goodbye - Formal) — Pronounced "owf vee-der-zay-en." Literally translating to "until we see again," this is the standard, polite way to say goodbye to a shopkeeper, hotel receptionist, or stranger. It is used during the day or evening.
*   **Tschüss** (Bye - Informal) — Pronounced "choos" (rhymes with "moose"). This is the most common informal goodbye. Interestingly, in many parts of Germany, shopkeepers will say "Tschüss" to customers even if the customer used "Auf Wiedersehen." It is casual, friendly, and ubiquitous.
*   **Bis bald** (See you soon) — Pronounced "bis balt." Used in both formal and informal contexts when you expect to see the person again shortly.

### Time-Specific Goodbyes

*   **Gute Nacht** (Good night) — Pronounced "goo-tuh nakht." Used only when someone is actually going to bed. It is not a general evening farewell.
*   **Guten Abend** can also be used as a farewell when leaving an evening event.

### The Regional Goodbyes

Just as with greetings, regional farewells are incredibly common.

*   **Ade** (Goodbye) — Pronounced "ah-day." Common in the Swabian region (around Stuttgart) and parts of Bavaria.
*   **Ciao** (Bye) — Pronounced "chow." Borrowed from Italian, this has become a very popular informal goodbye across all of Germany, especially among younger people.

## Introducing Yourself and Asking for Names

Once you have greeted someone, the next logical step in a conversation is introducing yourself. This is where the formal and informal distinction becomes crucial again.

### Asking for a Name

To ask someone for their name, you have two options depending on your relationship with the person.

*   **Wie heißen Sie?** (What is your name? - Formal) — Pronounced "vee hy-ssen zee." The verb *heißen* means "to be called." Use this with anyone you have just met in a professional or service context.
*   **Wie heißt du?** (What is your name? - Informal) — Pronounced "vee hy-sst doo." Use this with children, peers, or in casual social settings like a hostel bar.

### Giving Your Name

To respond, you use the same verb, *heißen*.

*   **Ich heiße...** (My name is... / I am called...) — Pronounced "ich hy-ssuh." Simply add your name to the end. For example, "Ich heiße Sarah."
*   **Mein Name ist...** (My name is...) — Pronounced "mine nah-muh ist." This is a slightly more formal alternative that directly mirrors the English structure.

### A Note on "Ich"

The word **Ich** (I) is one of the most frequently used words in the German language, and it contains that tricky "ch" sound we discussed earlier. Remember, it is not pronounced like the English word "itch." The "I" is short, and the "ch" is a soft, hissing sound made at the front of the roof of the mouth. It sounds closer to "ish," but with the tongue positioned differently.

## Putting It All Together: A Realistic Scenario

Imagine you have just arrived at your hotel in Munich. You approach the front desk to check in. Here is how your interaction might look, combining the pronunciation rules and vocabulary you have just learned.

**You:** *Guten Tag!* (Good day!)
**Receptionist:** *Guten Tag! Grüß Gott!* (Good day! May God greet you! — *Using the local Bavarian greeting to make you feel welcome.*)
**You:** *Wie heißt du?* — *Wait, no. This is a formal setting. You adjust.* *Wie heißen Sie?* (What is your name?)
**Receptionist:** *Ich heiße Herr Müller.* (My name is Mr. Müller. — *Notice the umlaut over the 'u'. Practice rounding your lips tightly for that sound.*)
**You:** *Mein Name ist Alex.* (My name is Alex.)
**Receptionist:** *Willkommen, Alex.* (Welcome, Alex.)

Later, you step out to grab a snack. You walk into a small shop. 

**You:** *Hallo!* (Hello!)
**Shopkeeper:** *Hallo!*
**You:** *Ein Brötchen, bitte.* (One roll, please. — *You point to the bread.*)
**Shopkeeper:** *Hier. Zwei Euro.* (Here. Two euros.)
**You:** *Danke.* (Thanks.)
**Shopkeeper:** *Tschüss!* (Bye!)
**You:** *Auf Wiedersehen!* (Goodbye! — *Or you could try the local "Ade" or the borrowed "Ciao".*)

By mastering just these basic sounds and phrases, you transform yourself from a tourist who simply points and speaks English into a traveler who makes an effort to engage with the local culture. Germans deeply appreciate this effort, and you will find that a simple, correctly pronounced "Guten Tag" opens doors—both literally and figuratively.

## Key Takeaways

*   **German is phonetic:** Letters almost always make the same sounds. Once you know the rules, you can pronounce almost any written word.
*   **Vowels are pure:** Do not glide German vowels. Pronounce them clearly, either short and clipped, or long and sustained.
*   **Watch out for consonants:** German **w** sounds like English "v", German **v** sounds like English "f", and German **z** sounds like "ts".
*   **Master the special characters:** The umlauts (ä, ö, ü) alter vowel sounds, and the Eszett (ß) is simply a sharp "s" sound.
*   **Greet by time of day:** Use *Guten Morgen*, *Guten Tag*, and *Guten Abend*. When in doubt, *Hallo* works everywhere.
*   **Know your goodbyes:** Use *Auf Wiedersehen* for formal situations and *Tschüss* for casual ones.
*   **Formal vs. Informal matters:** Use *Sie* (formal) with strangers and service workers, and *du* (informal) with friends and children. When asking a name, this means choosing between *Wie heißen Sie?* and *Wie heißt du?*

# Chapter 2: Polite Phrases and Simple Questions

## The Magic Words: Please, Thank You, and Excuse Me

You are standing in a busy Berlin train station. You accidentally bump into a stranger, and you need to get their attention to ask for the time. In English, you would instinctively say "excuse me" and "thank you." These words act as social lubricant, smoothing over awkward interactions and showing respect. 

In German, courtesy is highly valued, and having a handful of polite phrases ready will completely change the way locals interact with you. If you show you are making an effort to be polite, Germans will almost always warm up to you and go out of their way to help.

### Please and Thank You

The most essential polite phrase in German is **bitte**. It is incredibly versatile and translates to "please," "you're welcome," "here you go," and even "pardon?" 

Because *bitte* does so much heavy lifting, Germans often pair it with other words to be more specific:
*   **Bitte** – Please.
*   **Danke** – Thank you.
*   **Vielen Dank** – Many thanks / Thank you very much.
*   **Bitte schön** – You're very welcome (literally: "please beautiful"). You will also hear this used when handing someone an item, meaning "here you go."
*   **Danke schön** – Thank you very much (literally: "thank you beautiful"). 

If someone says *Danke schön* to you, the natural response is *Bitte schön*. It is a perfectly balanced exchange.

### Excuse Me

When you need to navigate through a crowd, get a server's attention at a restaurant, or apologize for a minor mistake, you need the phrase for "excuse me." German gives you two main options depending on exactly what you mean:

1.  **Entschuldigung** (en-SHOOL-di-goong): This is the most common, all-purpose "excuse me." Use it when bumping into someone, when trying to pass someone on a narrow sidewalk, or as a direct replacement for "sorry."
2.  **Verzeihung** (fer-TSY-oong): This is a slightly shorter, highly practical alternative. It is often shouted out in crowded places when you are trying to weave through a crowd ("Excuse me, coming through!"). 

*Pronunciation note:* Remember from Chapter 1 that the German **v** is usually pronounced like the English **f**. Therefore, *Verzeihung* starts with an "f" sound. Also, the **ie** in *Verzeihung*? Wait—that's an **ei**! Recall that **ei** is pronounced like the English word "eye." So, *Verzeihung* sounds like "fer-TSY-oong."

## The Great Divide: Formal 'Sie' vs. Informal 'du'

In Chapter 1, we briefly touched on the concepts of formal and informal greetings (like choosing between *Hallo* and *Guten Tag*). Now, we must look at the actual words you use to address people. 

English has a major convenience: the word "you" works for everyone. You can say "you" to a toddler, your best friend, your boss, or the President. German, like many other European languages, splits "you" into two distinct words based on social distance. 

### When to use 'du' (Informal)

The word **du** (pronounced "doo") is the informal "you." You use it with:
*   Family members
*   Friends and peers your own age
*   Children (typically under the age of 15)
*   Pets

Using *du* implies familiarity and closeness. If you use *du* with someone you have just met, it is the equivalent of walking up to them and calling them by their first name without an introduction. 

### When to use 'Sie' (Formal)

The word **Sie** (pronounced "zee") is the formal "you." You use it with:
*   Strangers
*   Shopkeepers, waiters, and hotel staff
*   People in positions of authority (police, train conductors)
*   Anyone noticeably older than you
*   Business colleagues you haven't been explicitly told to be informal with

Notice that *Sie* is always capitalized in writing. This helps you distinguish it from the word *sie*, which (with a lowercase 's') means "she" or "they." When in doubt as a traveler, always default to *Sie*. It is better to be slightly overly formal than to accidentally disrespect someone by being too casual.

### The Transition: "Shall we say du?"

The shift from *Sie* to *du* is a rite of passage in German culture. It is not automatic; it must be explicitly offered by the older person or the person of higher rank. When this happens, they will say something like, *"Wir können uns duzen"* (We can say *du* to each other) or simply, *"Sollen wir 'du' sagen?"* (Shall we say *du*?). 

Until that magic moment occurs, stick to *Sie*. As a traveler, you will likely use *Sie* 95% of the time.

## Asking for Help: Language Survival Phrases

One of the most important things you can do as a traveler in Germany is to honestly communicate your language abilities. Attempting a few words in German shows respect, but trying to fake your way through a complex conversation will only lead to confusion. 

Here are your survival phrases. Memorize these, as you will use them daily:

*   **Ich spreche kein Deutsch.** – I don't speak German. (Literally: I speak no German.)
*   **Sprechen Sie Englisch?** – Do you speak English?

Let's break down the pronunciation and structure of these vital sentences.

### "Ich spreche kein Deutsch."

*   **Ich** (ikh): The German word for "I". The **ch** here is the soft, hissing sound from the back of your throat, like the "h" in "huge" or the "ch" in the Scottish word "loch."
*   **spreche** (SHPREKH-uh): The verb "speak."
*   **kein** (kyn): The word for "no" or "none" when referring to a noun. (The **ei** is pronounced like the English "eye".)
*   **Deutsch** (doytsh): The German language. (Another **ei** sound, followed by the **sch** sound from Chapter 1).

### "Sprechen Sie Englisch?"

Notice the word **Sie** tucked into the middle of this question. In German, questions are formed by putting the verb first, followed by the subject. 
*   Verb: **Sprechen** (Speak)
*   Subject: **Sie** (You, formal)

Because you used *Sie*, you are politely asking a stranger if they speak English. If you were asking a friend, you would say, *Sprichst du Englisch?* But for your travels, stick to *Sprechen Sie Englisch?*

**A Realistic Scenario:**
You walk into a pharmacy in Munich looking for a specific type of painkiller. The pharmacist greets you. 
Pharmacist: *Guten Tag!*
You: *Guten Tag. Entschuldigung, ich spreche kein Deutsch. Sprechen Sie Englisch?*
Pharmacist: *Ja, a little bit. How can I help you?*

By leading with an apology for not speaking German (*Entschuldigung, ich spreche kein Deutsch*) and politely asking if they speak English (*Sprechen Sie Englisch?*), you have shown respect for their culture and language. The pharmacist will almost always switch to English happily to assist you.

## Building Blocks: Simple Questions

Now that you know how to be polite and how to ask for an English safety net, let's look at the basic building blocks of asking questions. 

In English, we rely heavily on the verb "to be" and "to do" to form questions (e.g., "Where *is* the station?" or "What *do* you want?"). In German, it is much simpler. To ask a basic question, you just take a question word (like *who*, *what*, or *where*) and put the verb right next to it, followed by the subject.

Let's look at the four most important question words for a beginner.

### 1. What? (Was?)

The German word for "what" is **was** (pronounced "vas"). Remember, the German **v** sounds like the English **f**... wait, no! *Was* starts with a **w**. As noted in Chapter 1, the German **w** is pronounced like the English **v**. 

*   **Was ist das?** – What is that?
    *   *Was* (What) + *ist* (is) + *das* (that). 
    *   Pronunciation: *vas ist das?*

### 2. Where? (Wo?)

The German word for "where" is **wo** (pronounced "vo"). Again, the **w** becomes a **v** sound. 

*   **Wo ist das Hotel?** – Where is the hotel?
    *   Pronunciation: *vo ist das ho-tel?*

*Note: German also has a word for "where to" (**wohin**) and "where from" (**woher**). For now, focus on the basic **wo** for simple location questions.*

### 3. Who? (Wer?)

Here is where things get tricky for English speakers. The German word for "who" is **wer** (pronounced "vair"). Notice the vowel shift: English "where" sounds like German "wer," and English "who" starts with a "w" in German. Be careful not to mix these up!

*   **Wer ist da?** – Who is there?
    *   Pronunciation: *vair ist da?*

### 4. When? (Wann?)

Fortunately, the German word for "when" is **wann** (pronounced "van"), which is very close to the English word, just with the w-to-v shift.

*   **Wann kommt der Zug?** – When is the train coming? (Literally: When comes the train?)
    *   Pronunciation: *van komt dair tsoog?*

## Putting It All Together: A Day in Munich

Imagine you have just arrived at Marienplatz, the central square in Munich. You are looking for a specific museum, but you are turned around. You see a well-dressed older gentleman standing on the corner. Here is how your interaction might go, utilizing everything you have learned so far:

1.  **You approach politely:** *Entschuldigung!* (Excuse me!)
2.  **The man looks up:** *Ja?* (Yes?)
3.  **You state your language limitation:** *Guten Tag. Ich spreche kein Deutsch. Sprechen Sie Englisch?* (Good day. I don't speak German. Do you speak English?)
4.  **The man smiles and nods:** *Ja, I speak English. How can I help?*
5.  **You ask your 'where' question:** *Thank you. Wo ist das Museum?* (Where is the museum?)
6.  **The man gives you directions in English.**
7.  **You express gratitude:** *Vielen Dank!*
8.  **The man responds:** *Bitte schön.* (You're welcome.)

In this short interaction, you successfully used a polite attention-getter (*Entschuldigung*), a formal greeting (*Guten Tag*), the formal "you" (*Sie*), a survival phrase (*Ich spreche kein Deutsch*), a question word (*Wo*), and polite gratitude phrases (*Vielen Dank* / *Bitte schön*). 

## Common Pitfalls for Beginners

As you practice these phrases, keep an eye out for these common mistakes:

*   **Forgetting the w-to-v shift:** It is incredibly common for beginners to say "was" with an English 'w' sound (like a buzzing bee). Remember, it's "vas." Similarly, "wo" is "vo." 
*   **Using 'du' with service staff:** When you walk into a bakery or a shop, the person serving you is a professional providing a service. Even if they look young, default to *Sie*. Using *du* can come across as overly familiar or even condescending.
*   **Mixing up 'wer' and 'wo':** English speakers frequently point at a person and ask "Wo ist das?" (Where is that?) when they mean "Who is that?" (*Wer ist das?*). Keep your *wo* for locations and your *wer* for people.
*   **Pronouncing 'ch' too harshly:** In words like *Ich* (I) and *nicht* (not), the **ch** is soft. Do not hack it up like you are clearing your throat. Think of the gentle hiss in the word "human." 

## Key Takeaways

*   **Bitte** and **Danke** are your most important words. *Bitte* means "please" and "you're welcome," while *Danke* means "thank you." Pair them as *Bitte schön* and *Danke schön* for extra politeness.
*   **Entschuldigung** is your go-to word for "excuse me" or "sorry" when navigating crowds or getting attention.
*   German has two words for "you." Use **du** for friends, family, and children. Use **Sie** (always capitalized) for strangers, service workers, and authority figures. When in doubt, use *Sie*.
*   To survive language barriers, master the phrase **Ich spreche kein Deutsch** (I don't speak German) and **Sprechen Sie Englisch?** (Do you speak English?).
*   Basic questions are formed by putting the question word first, followed by the verb, then the subject: **Was ist das?** (What is that?), **Wo ist das?** (Where is that?), **Wer ist da?** (Who is there?), **Wann...?** (When...?).
*   Remember the pronunciation shift: German **w** sounds like English **v** (so *wo* = vo, *was* = vas, *wer* = vair).

# Chapter 3: Numbers, Money, and Paying

## Counting from 1 to 12: The Foundation

You are standing in a bustling bakery in Berlin. The display is filled with pretzels, pastries, and loaves of bread. You point to a Schoko-Croissant and hold up two fingers. The baker nods and says, "Das macht vier Euro." 

To survive and thrive in German-speaking countries, you need to understand numbers. Whether you are buying a snack, paying for a hotel, or figuring out if you got the right change, numbers are the backbone of travel transactions. 

Let’s start at the very beginning. In German, counting from 1 to 12 is highly logical, but the pronunciation requires a little attention. 

Here are your first numbers:

1. **eins** (sounds like "eyens")
2. **zwei** (sounds like "tsvy") - Remember from Chapter 1 that the German **z** is pronounced like a sharp English "ts".
3. **drei** (sounds like "dry")
4. **vier** (sounds like "feer" - the **v** in German is usually pronounced like the English "f")
5. **fünf** (sounds like "fuhnf")
6. **sechs** (sounds like "zeks")
7. **sieben** (sounds like "zee-ben")
8. **acht** (sounds like "ahkt" - the **ch** here is a harsh, throaty sound, as introduced in Chapter 1)
9. **neun** (sounds like "noyn")
10. **zehn** (sounds like "tsayn")
11. **elf** (sounds like "elf")
12. **zwölf** (sounds like "tsvelf" - using the **ö** umlaut sound from Chapter 1)

Take a moment to practice these. They are the building blocks for every other number you will learn.

## The Teens: 13 to 19

Once you hit thirteen, German introduces a pattern that you will see repeated throughout the number system. Instead of saying "thirteen" (three-ten), German says "drei-zehn" (three-ten). 

To form the teens, you take the numbers 3 through 9 and add **-zehn** to the end of them.

*   13: **dreizehn**
*   14: **vierzehn**
*   15: **fünfzehn**
*   16: **sechzehn** *(Notice that the "s" in sechs drops off, making it sech-zehn, not sechs-zehn)*
*   17: **siebzehn** *(Similarly, "sieben" shortens to "sieb-zehn")*
*   18: **achtzehn**
*   19: **neunzehn**

## The Tens: 20 to 100

Now let’s jump to the tens. Just like the teens, the tens follow a predictable pattern based on the numbers you already know. 

*   20: **zwanzig**
*   30: **dreißig** *(Notice the special German letter **ß**, or "Eszett". It is simply a double "s" sound.)*
*   40: **vierzig**
*   50: **fünfzig**
*   60: **sechzig** *(Again, the "s" drops from "sechs")*
*   70: **siebzig** *(Again, "sieben" shortens to "sieb-")*
*   80: **achtzig**
*   90: **neunzig**
*   100: **(ein)hundert**

## The Big Reversal: Numbers 21 to 99

Here is where German throws English speakers a curveball. In English, we say "twenty-one" (tens first, then units). In German, you flip it. You say the unit first, followed by the word **und** (and), followed by the ten.

Let’s look at the formula:
**[Unit] + und + [Ten]**

*   21: einundzwanzig (one-and-twenty)
*   22: zweiundzwanzig (two-and-twenty)
*   33: dreiunddreißig (three-and-thirty)
*   44: vierundvierzig (four-and-forty)
*   55: fünfundfünfzig (five-and-fifty)
*   67: siebenundsechzig (seven-and-sixty)
*   89: neunundachtzig (nine-and-eighty)
*   99: neunundneunzig (nine-and-ninety)

When you say these out loud, they sound like one long, run-together word: **ein-und-zwanzig**. When writing them, they are always written as a single, capitalized (if at the start of a sentence) word: *Einundzwanzig*. 

This "reverse order" takes a little getting used to, but it is entirely consistent. If you know your numbers 1 to 9 and your tens (20, 30, 40, etc.), you can build any number up to 99.

## Understanding Money in German-Speaking Countries

Before you ask for a price, it helps to know what you are paying with. Germany, Austria, and the German-speaking parts of Switzerland all use the **Euro (€)**. 

*   **Germany and Austria:** Both use the Euro. Prices are usually displayed with a comma instead of a period for decimals. For example, €4.50 is written as **4,50 €**. 
*   **Switzerland:** While Switzerland is not in the European Union, it is surrounded by Euro-using countries. The official currency is the **Swiss Franc (CHF)**. However, many shops near the border or in tourist areas will accept Euros (though you will likely get your change in Francs). 

The word for money in German is **Geld** (sounds like "gelt"). When you pay, you will be dealing with cash (**Bargeld**) or a card (**Karte**). 

One important cultural note: Germany is historically a cash-forward society. While card payments have become much more common since 2020, you will still find many small bakeries, markets, or beer gardens that only accept cash (*nur Bargeld*). Always keep some physical Euros on you.

## Asking for the Price

You see a beautiful wooden clock in a shop window in Munich. You want to know how much it costs. There are a few ways to ask, depending on how polite or direct you want to be.

### The Direct Question
The simplest way to ask "How much is that?" is:
**Wie viel kostet das?** 
*(How much costs that?)*

If you are pointing at multiple items (like three pretzels), the verb changes slightly:
**Wie viel kosten die?**
*(How much cost those?)*

### The Polite Question
In Chapter 2, we covered the importance of formal vs. informal interactions. When speaking to a shop assistant, you should use the formal approach. To ask "How much does that cost?", you can say:
**Was kostet das, bitte?**
*(What costs that, please?)*

Or, if you want to be very polite:
**Entschuldigung, wie viel kostet das?**
*(Excuse me, how much costs that?)*

### Understanding the Answer
When the shopkeeper replies, they will likely just say the number and the currency. 

*   "Das kostet fünf Euro." (That costs five Euros.)
*   "Zehn Euro fünfzig." (Ten Euros fifty [cents].)

If the price is something like €3.20, they will say:
*   "Drei Euro zwanzig." (Three Euros twenty.)

## A Real-World Scenario: At the Kiosk

Let’s put it together. You walk into a small shop (*Kiosk*) in Vienna to buy a bottle of water and a newspaper.

**You:** *Guten Tag. Entschuldigung, wie viel kostet das Wasser?* (Hello. Excuse me, how much costs the water?)
**Shopkeeper:** *Das Wasser kostet zwei Euro.* (The water costs two Euros.)
**You:** *Und die Zeitung?* (And the newspaper?)
**Shopkeeper:** *Drei Euro fünfzig. Zusammen macht das fünf Euro fünfzig.* (Three Euros fifty. Together that makes five Euros fifty.)
**You:** *Hier sind sechs Euro.* (Here are six Euros.)
**Shopkeeper:** *Und fünfzig Cent zurück.* (And fifty cents back.)

In this scenario, the shopkeeper used the word **zusammen** (together) to give you the total. They also used the word **zurück** (back) to indicate your change.

## Paying by Card or Cash

When it is time to pay, you will need to communicate how you want to settle up. 

### Asking to Pay
To say "I would like to pay," you use a phrase that relies on the polite, formal structures we learned in Chapter 2:
**Ich möchte bezahlen.**
*(I would like to pay.)*

If you want to be even more direct and say "I am paying," you can simply say:
**Ich zahle.**
*(I am paying.)*

### Card vs. Cash
To specify how you are paying, you just add the method to the end of the phrase.

*   **Ich zahle mit Karte.** (I am paying with card.)
*   **Ich möchte mit Karte bezahlen.** (I would like to pay with card.)
*   **Ich zahle bar.** (I am paying cash.)
*   **Ich möchte mit Bargeld bezahlen.** (I would like to pay with cash.)

If you are at a restaurant or a counter, you can also just hold up your card and ask:
**Kann ich mit Karte zahlen?**
*(Can I pay with card?)*

The cashier will respond with **Ja** (Yes) or **Nein, nur Bargeld** (No, only cash). 

### The PIN Number
If you are paying by card, the machine might prompt you for your PIN. In German, the PIN number is simply called **die PIN** (pronounced like the English word "pin"). If the cashier needs to tell you to enter your PIN, they will say:
**Bitte PIN eingeben.**
*(Please enter PIN.)*

## Requesting a Receipt

In Germany and Austria, keeping your receipts (*die Quittung* or *der Bon*) is highly recommended. If you are traveling for business, you absolutely need them for expense reports. Even as a tourist, having a receipt is useful if you need to return an item or if there is a discrepancy with your card statement.

To ask for a receipt, you can use one of the following phrases:

**Kann ich eine Quittung haben, bitte?**
*(Can I have a receipt, please?)*

**Brauche ich eine Quittung?** 
*(Do I need a receipt? - Sometimes cashiers will ask you this to save paper. If you want it, reply "Ja, bitte".)*

A shorter, very common way to ask for a receipt, especially in quick-service environments like bakeries or fast food, is:
**Einen Bon, bitte.**
*(A receipt, please.)*

## Common Payment Vocabulary

To wrap up your financial transaction toolkit, here is a quick reference list of the most important words for paying in German:

*   **der Euro:** Euro
*   **der Cent:** Cent
*   **das Geld:** Money
*   **das Bargeld:** Cash
*   **die Karte:** Card (debit or credit)
*   **die Quittung / der Bon:** Receipt
*   **die Rechnung:** The bill (used in restaurants or hotels)
*   **bezahlen:** To pay
*   **kosten:** To cost
*   **teuer:** Expensive
*   **billig / günstig:** Cheap / inexpensive
*   **das Wechselgeld:** The change (the coins/bills you get back)

## Key Takeaways

*   **Number Structure:** German numbers 1-12 must be memorized. 13-19 are formed by adding **-zehn** to the numbers 3-9. The tens (20, 30, 40) end in **-zig** (or **-ßig** for 30).
*   **The Reversal Rule:** For numbers 21 through 99, German flips the English order. You say the single digit first, then **und** (and), then the ten. (e.g., 21 = einundzwanzig = one-and-twenty). 
*   **Asking Price:** The most direct way to ask how much something costs is **Wie viel kostet das?** 
*   **Paying:** To say you want to pay, use **Ich möchte bezahlen**. Specify your method by saying **mit Karte** (with card) or **bar / mit Bargeld** (cash).
*   **Cash is King:** While cards are widely accepted in cities, always carry **Bargeld** (cash), as many smaller shops or markets in Germany and Austria are cash-only.
*   **Receipts:** Always ask for a receipt by saying **Kann ich eine Quittung haben?** or simply **Einen Bon, bitte.**

# Chapter 4: Navigation and Public Transportation

## Finding Your Way: Asking for Key Locations

You step off the plane or train in Germany, drop your bags at your hotel, and are ready to explore. But first, you need to find the train station, locate a bathroom, and figure out how to get to your next destination. 

When asking where something is in German, the most important phrase you need is:
**Wo ist...?** (Where is...?)

To ask for a specific location, simply add the word for the place to the end of the question. 

Here are the essential locations you will need to find:

*   **der Bahnhof** (the train station) - *Pronounced: bahn-hohf*
*   **der Busbahnhof** (the bus station) - *Pronounced: boos-bahn-hohf*
*   **die Toilette** (the bathroom / toilet) - *Pronounced: twa-let-uh*. Note: In German, it is perfectly polite to ask for the "Toilette." Do not look for a "bathroom" (Badezimmer), as Germans reserve that word for the room in a house where you take a shower.
*   **der Ausgang** (the exit) - *Pronounced: ows-gahng*
*   **der Eingang** (the entrance) - *Pronounced: yn-gahng*
*   **die Apotheke** (the pharmacy) - *Pronounced: ah-po-teh-kuh*
*   **das Hotel** (the hotel) - *Pronounced: ho-tel*

### Putting It Together

If you want to be polite—which, as we learned in Module 2, goes a long way in Germany—you can combine your greeting with your question. 

*   *Guten Tag. Wo ist der Bahnhof?* (Hello. Where is the train station?)
*   *Entschuldigung, wo ist die Toilette?* (Excuse me, where is the bathroom?)

When Germans give you directions, they will use a few key prepositions. You don’t need to memorize every word they say, but listening for these specific keywords will tell you which way to go:

*   **links** (left)
*   **rechts** (right)
*   **geradeaus** (straight ahead)
*   **hier** (here)
*   **dort** (there)

If someone speaks too fast or gives you a complex set of directions, you can simply smile, say **Danke!** (Thank you!), and look for the signs. Fortunately, German public transportation signs are highly standardized and use clear international symbols.

## Buying a Ticket for Trains and Buses

Germany has an incredibly robust public transportation network. Whether you are taking a massive intercity train or a local city bus, the process of buying a ticket is quite similar. 

First, let’s look at the types of tickets you might need to ask for. 

*   **die Fahrkarte** (the ticket) - *Pronounced: fahr-kar-tuh*
*   **einfach** (one-way / single) - *Pronounced: yn-fakh*
*   **hin und zurück** (round-trip / there and back) - *Pronounced: hin oont tsoo-rook*
*   **der Erwachsene** (the adult) - *Pronounced: air-vahk-sen-uh*
*   **das Kind** (the child) - *Pronounced: kint*

### At the Ticket Machine

Most ticket machines in Germany have an English language option (usually marked with a British or American flag, or the word "EN"). However, if you want to practice your German, or the touch screen is uncooperative, here are the phrases you will see and need:

1.  **Fahrkarte kaufen** (Buy ticket)
2.  **Ziel eingeben** (Enter destination)
3.  **Bezahlen** (Pay)

As we covered in Module 3, you will need to know your numbers and how to pay. Most machines accept **Bargeld** (cash) and **Karte** (card). 

### Buying from a Person

If you are buying a ticket at a counter in a train station, you can use the following script. Let’s look at a concrete scenario where you want to buy a one-way ticket to Munich.

**You:** Guten Tag. Ich möchte eine Fahrkarte nach München, einfach. 
*(Hello. I would like a ticket to Munich, one-way.)*

**Ticket Agent:** Das macht 25 Euro. 
*(That makes 25 Euros.)*

**You:** Hier, bitte. *(Here, please - as you hand over your money or card)*

**Ticket Agent:** Vielen Dank. Hier ist Ihre Fahrkarte. Gute Reise!
*(Many thanks. Here is your ticket. Good trip!)*

**You:** Danke, gleichfalls! *(Thank you, same to you!)*

### Understanding the Zones

In many German cities, public transportation is divided into zones (**Zonen**). A standard ticket might be for **Zone A** (the city center), while traveling to the suburbs requires **Zone B** or **Zone C**. If you are unsure, you can always ask:

*   **Welche Zone brauche ich?** (Which zone do I need?)

## Asking for Departure and Arrival Times

To catch your train or bus, you need to know when it leaves and when it arrives. The German words for these concepts are wonderfully literal:

*   **die Abfahrt** (the departure) - Literally "the off-drive." Used for buses and trains.
*   **die Ankunft** (the arrival) - Literally "the on-come." 
*   **der Bahnsteig** (the platform / track) - Where you stand to catch a train.
*   **die Haltestelle** (the stop) - Where you stand to catch a bus or local tram.

### Asking the Questions

To ask when a train or bus leaves, use the phrase **Wann fährt...?** (When drives...?)

*   *Wann fährt der Zug?* (When does the train leave?)
*   *Wann fährt der Bus?* (When does the bus leave?)

To ask when it arrives, use **Wann kommt...?** (When comes...?)

*   *Wann kommt der Zug in München an?* (When does the train arrive in Munich?)

Notice the word **an** at the end of that sentence. In German, "to arrive" is a separable verb (*ankommen*), so the prefix **an** gets pushed to the very end of the sentence. You don't need to worry too much about the grammar rules right now; just memorize the phrase "Wann kommt... an?" as a single chunk.

### Reading the Departure Board

When you look up at the large yellow departure board (**Abfahrtstafel**) in a German train station, you will see a few key columns. Knowing what they mean will save you from missing your ride:

*   **Zeit** (Time) - When the vehicle departs.
*   **Ziel** (Destination) - The final stop on that line.
*   **Gleis** (Track) - The platform number where you need to stand. Note: On local trains, this might be called **Bahnsteig**.
*   **Produkt** (Product) - The type of train. 
    *   **ICE** (InterCity Express) - The high-speed trains.
    *   **RE** (Regional Express) - Regional trains that stop at medium-sized towns.
    *   **S-Bahn** - Local city trains (Stadtbahn).
    *   **Bus / Tram** - Local street transportation.

## Taking a Taxi

Sometimes, public transportation isn’t convenient, or you have heavy luggage. In Germany, you don't usually need to hail a taxi from the side of the road. You can find them at designated **Taxistände** (taxi stands) near train stations, airports, and major squares, or you can call one.

The word for taxi in German is simply **das Taxi** (pronounced *tux-see*). 

### Telling the Driver Your Destination

Telling a taxi driver where you want to go is one of the easiest interactions you will have in German. You just use the phrase **Ich möchte nach...** (I would like to go to...) or **Bitte zum...** (Please to the...).

Here is how a typical taxi ride begins:

**You:** Guten Tag. Ich möchte zum Hotel Berlin, bitte.
*(Hello. I would like to go to the Hotel Berlin, please.)*

**Driver:** Alles klar. 
*(All clear / Got it.)*

If you have a specific address, you can say the street name and number. Remember from Module 3 that Germans say numbers in a specific way, so "fünfzehn" (15) is different from "fünfzig" (50). Speak clearly to avoid ending up at the wrong address!

**You:** Bitte zur Goethestraße fünfzehn.
*(Please to Goethe Street 15.)*

### Useful Taxi Phrases

While the driver does the driving, a few phrases can help you control the journey:

*   **Bitte links.** (Please left.)
*   **Bitte rechts.** (Please right.)
*   **Bitte hier halten.** (Please stop here.)
*   **Können Sie bitte warten?** (Can you please wait?)

When you arrive, the meter will show the price. As we practiced in Module 3, you can ask **Kann ich mit Karte zahlen?** (Can I pay with card?) if you don't have cash. 

## A Realistic Scenario: Navigating the Station

Let’s put it all together. You have just arrived in the city of Cologne (Köln) by train. You need to use the bathroom, buy a ticket to the airport, and find your platform. 

**Step 1: Finding the Bathroom**
You approach a station employee.
**You:** Entschuldigung, wo ist die Toilette?
**Employee:** Dort drüben, links.
*(Over there, on the left.)*
**You:** Danke schön!

**Step 2: Buying the Ticket**
You walk to the ticket machine. You select German to practice. 
You press **Fahrkarte kaufen**, then enter your destination: **Flughafen Köln/Bonn** (Cologne/Bonn Airport). 
The screen reads: **12,50 Euro**.
You insert your card, enter your PIN, and the machine dispenses your **Fahrkarte**.

**Step 3: Finding the Platform**
You look up at the yellow departure board. You scan the **Ziel** column until you find "Flughafen Köln/Bonn". You look across the row to the **Gleis** column. It says **Gleis 7**. 

You walk to Platform 7. As you wait, you check the digital sign above the platform to confirm the **Abfahrt** (departure) time. It reads **14:15**. 

When the train arrives, you look for the door, which will be marked with the word **Einstieg** (boarding/entry). You step on, find a seat, and you are on your way!

## Key Takeaways

*   **Wo ist...?** is your master key for finding locations like the Bahnhof (train station), Toilette (bathroom), or Ausgang (exit).
*   Listen for the directional words **links** (left), **rechts** (right), and **geradeaus** (straight ahead) when locals give you directions.
*   To buy a ticket (**die Fahrkarte**), specify if you want **einfach** (one-way) or **hin und zurück** (round-trip). 
*   **Wann fährt... ab?** asks when something departs, and **Wann kommt... an?** asks when it arrives.
*   Look for **Zeit** (time), **Ziel** (destination), and **Gleis** (track) on the yellow departure boards to find your train.
*   In a taxi, use **Ich möchte zum...** (I would like to go to...) followed by your destination to tell the driver exactly where you need to go.
